document: DNS Tool — Zone Health Golden Rules
copyright: Copyright (c) 2024-2026 IT Help San Diego Inc.

overview.purpose: Machine-readable specification of DNS Tool's Zone Health advisory logic. These rules encode RFC-mandated behaviors that govern how DNS Tool flags policy signals, computes structural scores, classifies zone profiles, and compares zone-to-live drift. All rules are locked by automated golden-rule tests (health_test.go) — if a test fails, the code change is wrong, not the test.

zone_profiles.description: Zone Health classifies every uploaded zone file into one of five profiles. Profile classification determines which policy signals are emitted.

profiles.name: Delegation-Only
profiles.description: TLD/registry zones or parent zones that delegate to child zones. Contains only SOA, NS, and DS records without address or email records.
profiles.detection_logic: DS records present AND no A/AAAA AND no MX/SPF/DMARC/DKIM; OR SOA+NS present with no address/email/DS records.

profiles.name: Full-Service
profiles.description: Standard domain zone serving both address records and email infrastructure.
profiles.detection_logic: Has A or AAAA records AND has MX/SPF/DMARC/DKIM.

profiles.name: Web-Only
profiles.description: Zone contains address records but no email infrastructure. Email may be managed externally.
profiles.detection_logic: Has A or AAAA records AND no MX/SPF/DMARC/DKIM.
examples: Landing page with A record, no MX

profiles.name: Email-Only
profiles.description: Zone contains email records but no address records. Web hosting may use CNAME or external service.
profiles.detection_logic: No A or AAAA records AND has MX/SPF/DMARC/DKIM.
examples: Domain with MX but web via CNAME

profiles.name: Minimal
profiles.description: Zone contains limited record types that do not fit other profiles.
profiles.detection_logic: Fallback when no other profile matches.

golden_rules.name: SPF Absence Always Flagged for Non-Delegation Zones
golden_rules.rule: When a zone file lacks an SPF record (TXT containing 'v=spf1'), it MUST be flagged as 'missing' for ALL zone profiles EXCEPT Delegation-Only.
golden_rules.rationale: Attackers send FROM a domain — they do not need the domain to receive mail. A domain without SPF allows any server on the internet to send email claiming to be from that domain. This applies even to parked domains, web-only domains, and domains with zero email infrastructure.

signal_emitted.label: SPF
signal_emitted.detail: No SPF record — any server can claim to send email as this domain (RFC 7208)

golden_rules.name: DMARC Absence Always Flagged for Non-Delegation Zones
golden_rules.rule: When a zone file lacks a DMARC record (_dmarc.* TXT containing 'v=DMARC1'), it MUST be flagged as 'missing' for ALL zone profiles EXCEPT Delegation-Only.
golden_rules.rationale: Without a DMARC policy, receiving mail servers have no enforcement instructions for spoofed email claiming to be from this domain. Even domains that never intend to send email need a DMARC reject policy (v=DMARC1; p=reject) to protect against impersonation.

signal_emitted.label: DMARC
signal_emitted.detail: No DMARC policy — receiving servers have no spoofing policy to enforce (RFC 7489)

golden_rules.name: Delegation-Only Suppresses SPF/DMARC Signals
golden_rules.rule: TLD and delegation-only zones (containing only SOA, NS, DS records) MUST NOT flag SPF or DMARC as missing. These policies do not apply at the TLD level.
golden_rules.rationale: SPF and DMARC are domain-level email policies. TLD zones (.com, .net) delegate to child zones — they do not send or receive email. Flagging SPF/DMARC absence for a TLD zone would be a false positive.
golden_rules.signal_emitted: No SPF or DMARC signals emitted for Delegation-Only zones

golden_rules.name: DANE/TLSA Absence Never Flagged
golden_rules.rule: DANE/TLSA absence MUST NEVER be flagged as 'missing' for ANY zone profile, including Full-Service zones with MX records.
golden_rules.rationale: TLSA records are per-service records (e.g., _443._tcp.host, _25._tcp.mail) and are typically managed outside the primary zone file, often by the service operator or automation. Flagging their absence would produce false positives for the vast majority of zones.
golden_rules.signal_emitted: No DANE/TLSA signal emitted when absent. Only 'detected' when present.

golden_rules.name: DANE/TLSA Presence Detected
golden_rules.rule: When TLSA records are present in the zone file, a 'detected' signal MUST be emitted with label 'TLSA/DANE'.
golden_rules.rationale: DANE presence is a positive security signal indicating the operator has deployed certificate pinning via DNS. This should be acknowledged.

signal_emitted.label: TLSA/DANE
signal_emitted.detail: DNS-Based Authentication of Named Entities records present

golden_rules.name: Policy Signals Never Affect Structural Score
golden_rules.rule: The structural score (0-100, computed from SOA/NS/address/TTL/duplicate checks) MUST NOT change based on the presence or absence of policy records (SPF, DMARC, CAA, TLSA, MX). A zone with SOA+NS+A must score identically whether or not it has SPF, DMARC, MX, CAA, or TLSA.
golden_rules.rationale: Structural score measures zone file correctness per RFC 1035 (SOA, NS, address records, TTL consistency, duplicates). Policy records are operational concerns — their presence indicates good practice but their absence does not make the zone structurally deficient. Mixing structural and operational scoring would conflate two distinct quality dimensions.

golden_rules.name: SPF Present Is Detected
golden_rules.rule: When a TXT record containing 'v=spf1' is present, a 'detected' signal MUST be emitted with label 'SPF'.
golden_rules.rationale: Positive acknowledgment that the domain has an SPF policy.

golden_rules.name: DMARC Present Is Detected
golden_rules.rule: When a TXT record at _dmarc.* containing 'v=DMARC1' is present, a 'detected' signal MUST be emitted with label 'DMARC'.
golden_rules.rationale: Positive acknowledgment that the domain has a DMARC enforcement policy.

golden_rules.name: Missing SPF Signal Must Include Risk Explanation
golden_rules.rule: When SPF is flagged as 'missing', the Detail field MUST be non-empty and explain the security risk (domain spoofability).
golden_rules.rationale: A bare 'missing' flag without context is not actionable. Operators need to understand why SPF matters to prioritize remediation.

advisory_protocols.description: Decision framework governing how DNS Tool decides whether and how to advise operators on policy signals. These protocols ensure consistent, RFC-grounded advisory output.

protocols.name: Profile-First Gating
protocols.description: Zone profile classification is the first gate. All signal emission decisions start by checking whether the zone is Delegation-Only. If yes, SPF/DMARC signals are suppressed entirely.
protocols.implementation: buildPolicySignals() checks isDelegation first; all SPF/DMARC missing branches are gated by !isDelegation.

protocols.name: Universal SPF/DMARC Warning
protocols.description: SPF and DMARC absence warnings apply to ALL non-delegation zones regardless of email infrastructure. The presence of MX records, DKIM selectors, or any email-related records does NOT gate SPF/DMARC warnings. This is because attackers spoof the FROM address — they do not need the victim domain to have MX records or any email infrastructure.
protocols.rfc_basis: RFC 7208 §2.1 (SPF applies to envelope sender), RFC 7489 §4 (DMARC applies to header From).

protocols.name: Email Intent Gates DKIM Info Only
protocols.description: DKIM absence is flagged as 'info' (not 'missing') and ONLY when email intent is detected (MX, SPF, DMARC, or DKIM records present). DKIM selectors in zone files are unusual — most email providers manage DKIM externally. Therefore DKIM absence is informational, not a warning.
protocols.implementation: emailIntent := HasMX || HasSPF || HasDMARC || HasDKIM; DKIM info signal gated by emailIntent && !isDelegation.

protocols.name: Web Intent Gates CAA Info Only
protocols.description: CAA absence is flagged as 'info' (not 'missing') and ONLY when web intent is detected (A or AAAA records present). CAA restricts which CAs can issue certificates — relevant only when the domain resolves to an address.
protocols.implementation: webIntent := HasA || HasAAAA; CAA info signal gated by webIntent && !isDelegation.

protocols.name: DANE/TLSA Is Detect-Only
protocols.description: DANE/TLSA is a per-service record (_443._tcp, _25._tcp). Its absence is never flagged because it is typically managed by service operators, not zone administrators. Only its presence is acknowledged as a 'detected' signal.
protocols.rfc_basis: RFC 6698 — TLSA records are tied to specific port/protocol combinations.

protocols.name: Structural vs Operational Separation
protocols.description: Structural score measures zone file correctness (SOA presence, NS redundancy, address records, SOA timer compliance, TTL consistency, duplicate detection). Policy/security signals (SPF, DMARC, DKIM, CAA, DANE) are reported separately and NEVER affect the structural score. This ensures the structural score reflects RFC 1035 compliance, not operational best practices.
protocols.implementation: computeStructuralScore() operates only on StructuralChecks (SOA, NS, address, TTL, duplicates). PolicySignals are emitted by buildPolicySignals() — a completely separate code path.

protocols.name: Signal Status Taxonomy
protocols.description: Every PolicySignal has exactly one of three statuses: 'detected' (record found in zone), 'missing' (record absent and its absence is a security concern per RFC), 'info' (record absent but its absence is informational, not a direct security risk). There is no 'warning' status for policy signals — structural checks use severity levels (critical, warning, info) but policy signals use the status taxonomy.

structural_scoring.description: How the structural score (0-100) is computed from RFC compliance checks.
structural_scoring.weight_formula: score = (sum of weights for passing checks) / (sum of all weights) × 100

drift_engine.description: The Drift Engine compares an operator's zone file (declared/intended state) against live DNS query results (actual deployed state). The term 'drift' follows established infrastructure-as-code convention (Terraform, CloudFormation, Ansible, NIST SP 800-128 configuration baseline deviation).
drift_engine.terminology_justification: Configuration drift is the industry-standard term for actual-state-vs-declared-state divergence. NIST SP 800-128 defines configuration baselines; drift is deviation from that baseline. Terraform uses 'drift detection' for the same concept. The zone file IS the declared state; live DNS IS the actual deployed state.

drift_categories.description: Record exists in zone file but was not observed in live DNS. May indicate a record that has not yet propagated, or a record that is only authoritative.
Record observed in live DNS but not present in zone file. May indicate an out-of-band change, dynamic DNS update, or provider-injected record.
Record exists in both zone and live DNS but the RDATA values differ.
Record exists in both with identical RDATA but different TTL values. Common due to resolver caching (live TTL reflects remaining cache time, not authoritative TTL).

known_false_positive_edges.description: Some DNS providers (Cloudflare, Route 53) synthesize A/AAAA records at the apex from CNAME targets. Zone file shows CNAME; live DNS shows A records.
known_false_positive_edges.mitigation: Documented in UI as a known provider behavior.

known_false_positive_edges.description: RRSIG, NSEC, NSEC3 records are generated by the signing process and typically not in the zone file.
known_false_positive_edges.mitigation: extractLiveRecords() limits comparison to basic types (A, AAAA, NS, CNAME, MX, TXT, SOA, CAA) — DNSSEC types are excluded.

known_false_positive_edges.description: Live DNS TTLs reflect remaining cache time at the resolver, not the authoritative TTL. A record with TTL 3600 may appear as TTL 1234 if queried mid-cache.
known_false_positive_edges.mitigation: TTL differences are reported as 'ttl_only' — a distinct, lower-severity category.

drift_engine.comparison_scope: Apex-level basic record types only: A, AAAA, NS, CNAME, MX, TXT, SOA, CAA. Subdomain records and DNSSEC types are excluded from drift comparison.

soa_timer_analysis.description: SOA timer values are validated against RFC 1912 §2.2 recommendations.

checks.rfc: RFC 1912 §2.2: retry should be less than refresh
RFC 1912 §2.2: expire must be greater than refresh
RFC 2308 §5 recommends 1–3 hours for most zones

checks.note: Serial 0 is technically valid but suggests zone has never been updated. YYYYMMDDNN format recommended.
